content.content:
- content: 'Academic recovery plans maintained through L&S advising are not automatically

    transmitted to Financial Aid and Scholarships.


    A financial-aid review case records the current case lifecycle, response

    window, and deadline. The case record does not itself establish which public

    policy applies or which remedy is appropriate.


    Use the current official Financial Aid policy to determine the applicable

    process, required materials, required reviews, submission channel, and the

    effect of enrollment level on any resulting aid eligibility or award amount.

    '

args.query: site:financialaid.berkeley.edu satisfactory academic progress appeal pace 67% financial aid review case fall 2026

content.content:
content: "# Web Document\n\n## Metadata\n\n- title: Satisfactory Academic Progress\
  \ | Financial Aid & Scholarships\n- url: https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/appeals-special-circumstances/satisfactory-academic-progress/\n\
  - source: UC Berkeley Financial Aid & Scholarships\n- domain: financialaid.berkeley.edu\n\
  \n## Content\n\n# Satisfactory Academic Progress\n\nFinancial-aid regulations require\
  \ students to meet Satisfactory Academic Progress (SAP) requirements to remain eligible\
  \ for financial aid. UC Berkeley evaluates academic records after each academic\
  \ year. The status determined at the annual evaluation controls financial-aid eligibility\
  \ for the upcoming academic year, including summer when applicable.\n\nSAP is a\
  \ financial-aid eligibility standard. Its requirements are related to, but distinct\
  \ from, a college's academic-standing process.\n\n## Undergraduate SAP requirements\n\
  \nUndergraduate students are evaluated in three areas:\n\n1. **Cumulative GPA:**\
  \ The student must maintain a cumulative GPA of at least 2.0.\n2. **Pace of progression:**\
  \ The student must successfully complete at least 67 percent of all units attempted\
  \ cumulatively, including accepted transfer units.\n3. **Maximum timeframe:** Attempted\
  \ units may not exceed 150 percent of the published program length. For a 120-unit\
  \ undergraduate degree, this generally means no more than 180 attempted units.\n\
  \nThe pace calculation is cumulative. It is calculated by dividing successfully\
  \ completed units by attempted units.\n\nThe 67 percent completion requirement continues\
  \ to apply even when a student has been approved for a Reduced Course Load by the\
  \ college or the Disabled Students Program. A Reduced Course Load changes the approved\
  \ semester enrollment level; it does not remove the cumulative SAP pace requirement.\n\
  \nPass/No Pass courses are included in the SAP calculation. A passing grade counts\
  \ as attempted and completed, while a non-passing grade counts as attempted but\
  \ not completed.\n\nSome courses and enrollment outcomes count as attempted units\
  \ but not completed units. These include repeated courses in which the student previously\
  \ received a passing grade, courses dropped after the applicable deadline, units\
  \ attempted in a semester from which the student withdrew after the deadline, and\
  \ failed or incomplete courses. These outcomes can lower the cumulative completion\
  \ percentage.\n\n## When a SAP appeal applies\n\nA SAP Appeal is relevant when a\
  \ student is ineligible for financial aid because the student is not meeting the\
  \ SAP requirements and extenuating circumstances affected academic progress. The\
  \ appeal asks UC Berkeley to reconsider financial-aid eligibility.\n\nSubmission\
  \ of an appeal does not guarantee approval.\n\nWhen a SAP Appeal is required, an\
  \ appeal is made available to the student through the university's designated process.\
  \ Students should use the assigned institutional appeal and review it for the applicable\
  \ deadline and required documentation. An incomplete appeal may require follow-up\
  \ before a decision can be made.\n\nFor 2026\u201327, the published priority dates\
  \ include September 18, 2026 for Fall 2026 and February 12, 2027 for Spring 2027.\
  \ Appeals may be submitted after a priority date, but a complete appeal must be\
  \ received before the end of the term being appealed.\n\n## Preparing the appeal\n\
  \nA complete appeal normally includes:\n\n- A letter explaining the circumstances\
  \ that prevented the student from meeting SAP.\n- An explanation of what has changed\
  \ and why the student can now follow the academic plan.\n- Specific steps and resources\
  \ the student will use to improve.\n- Supporting documentation for the circumstances,\
  \ when reasonably available.\n- The required Academic Plan.\n\nFor an undergraduate\
  \ student, the Academic Plan accompanying the appeal must be reviewed, approved,\
  \ and signed by the College Adviser. A declared Letters and Science major also needs\
  \ review and approval from the Undergraduate Major Adviser. A DSP specialist must\
  \ review the plan when required for a DSP Reduced Course Load.\n\nThe academic plan\
  \ is a required component of the appeal. Completing or discussing an academic recovery\
  \ plan does not by itself change the SAP determination or restore financial-aid\
  \ eligibility.\n\n## Submission and review\n\nSAP Appeals are submitted through\
  \ the Berkeley Financial Aid Forms process. The student completes and signs the\
  \ electronic appeal, uploads the supporting documentation and signed Academic Plan,\
  \ and completes the submission steps.\n\nAppeal decisions may take several weeks\
  \ after a complete submission. If an appeal is approved, the approval message states\
  \ the conditions the student must satisfy in later terms. Berkeley reviews progress\
  \ before additional aid is disbursed. A student considering a course-load change\
  \ after approval should discuss the change with both academic advising and financial\
  \ aid because it may affect the approved plan.\n\nIf the appeal is denied, or if\
  \ the student withdraws before approval, the student remains responsible for costs\
  \ incurred.\n"
metadata: {}
title: Satisfactory Academic Progress | Financial Aid & Scholarships
url: https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/appeals-special-circumstances/satisfactory-academic-progress/

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Academic Notice | L&S Advising

  - url: https://lsadvising.berkeley.edu/academic-difficulty/academic-notice

  - source: UC Berkeley L&S Advising

  - domain: lsadvising.berkeley.edu


  ## Content


  # Academic Notice


  Academic Notice is an academic-standing status used by the College of Letters and
  Science when a student''s grades indicate that additional planning and support are
  needed. A student may be placed on Academic Notice after a Fall or Spring semester
  when the term GPA is below 1.5, the cumulative UC GPA is below 2.0, or no letter
  grades are earned during the semester.


  Academic Notice concerns academic standing. It gives the student an opportunity
  to make positive academic progress during the next Fall or Spring semester of enrollment.
  Students who do not return to good standing by the end of that notice term may be
  required to take a break from UC Berkeley before seeking to return.


  ## Recommended steps


  Students placed on Academic Notice should:


  1. Make a plan for addressing the factors that affected their academic performance.

  2. Review communications from L&S and monitor university email.

  3. Work with an L&S College Adviser to plan the notice-term schedule and understand
  the grades needed to return to good standing.

  4. Meet with an adviser after midterm grades or earlier if there are concerns about
  keeping up in classes.

  5. Discuss available options before important academic deadlines if returning to
  good standing appears uncertain.


  An adviser can help the student assess academic challenges, identify support resources,
  and create a realistic course plan. The academic recovery process is not limited
  to one meeting; students are encouraged to monitor their progress throughout the
  semester.


  ## Transition to good standing


  To transition to Good Standing, a student must earn at least a 2.0 term GPA and
  reach at least a 2.0 cumulative UC GPA by the end of the next Fall or Spring semester
  of enrollment.


  During the Academic Notice term, courses that are available for a letter grade must
  be taken for a letter grade. Courses offered only as Pass/No Pass may still be taken.


  ## Unit load while on Academic Notice


  Students on Academic Notice are expected to remain enrolled in at least 13 units
  unless they have an approved Reduced Course Load.


  An approved Reduced Course Load allows a student to remain below the ordinary 13-unit
  L&S minimum for the approved term. A student considering a late course drop that
  would reduce enrollment below 13 units should discuss the academic consequences
  and available procedures with an L&S College Adviser.


  The effect of a lower unit load on financial aid is a separate question. Students
  should review the financial-aid rules that apply to their aid package rather than
  assuming that an L&S Reduced Course Load approval determines financial-aid eligibility
  or award amount.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Academic Notice | L&S Advising
url: https://lsadvising.berkeley.edu/academic-difficulty/academic-notice

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Eligibility for Financial Aid at UC Berkeley | Financial Aid & Scholarships

  - url: https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/eligibility/

  - source: UC Berkeley Financial Aid & Scholarships

  - domain: financialaid.berkeley.edu


  ## Content


  # Eligibility for Financial Aid at UC Berkeley


  UC Berkeley students must satisfy several general requirements to qualify for and
  maintain financial aid. The requirements include eligible student status, enrollment
  in an eligible degree program, satisfactory academic progress, and an adequate academic-term
  unit load.


  ## Enrollment and eligibility


  A regular student working toward a degree generally must be enrolled in at least
  six units in the academic term to meet the university''s minimum enrollment requirement
  for financial aid, unless the student is on the degree list for that term.


  For financial-aid purposes:


  - **12 or more units per semester** is considered full-time enrollment.

  - **6 through 11 units** can satisfy the general minimum enrollment requirement,
  but enrollment below 12 units may reduce eligibility for certain types of aid.

  - Meeting the six-unit minimum does not replace the requirement to maintain Satisfactory
  Academic Progress.


  The enrollment rule and the SAP rule are separate eligibility conditions. A student
  may be enrolled in enough units to meet the general minimum and still be ineligible
  because of SAP. Conversely, enrollment below 12 units does not necessarily mean
  that every type of aid is eliminated; the amount or availability of particular awards
  may change.


  ## Maintaining eligibility


  Students receiving aid are expected to:


  - Maintain Satisfactory Academic Progress.

  - Remain enrolled in the required number of units.

  - Verify participation in courses near the beginning of the semester.

  - Continue to have college or major requirements remaining for graduation.

  - Avoid owing a refund on a federal grant or being in default on a federal student
  loan.


  Enrollment changes can cause a financial-aid package to be recalculated. Students
  should not assume that an academic classification such as an approved Reduced Course
  Load automatically preserves the same award amount used for 12-unit full-time enrollment.


  The financial-aid effect depends on the student''s actual enrollment and the rules
  for each award. A student enrolled in nine units is above the general six-unit minimum
  but below the 12-unit full-time threshold, so some aid may be adjusted even when
  other eligibility requirements are satisfied.

  '
metadata: {}
title: Eligibility for Financial Aid at UC Berkeley | Financial Aid & Scholarships
url: https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/eligibility/

content.content:
Your fall aid is blocked because of **Satisfactory Academic Progress (SAP)**, not simply because you are on Academic Notice.

### What I found

- **Academic Notice:** Active for Fall 2026 after a prior-term GPA below 1.5 following a failed course.
- **Academic recovery plan:** In progress; student and College Adviser must complete it by **December 18, 2026**.
- **SAP result:** **Not met** because your cumulative completion rate is **64.9%**—48 completed units out of 74 attempted. Berkeley requires at least **67%**.
  - GPA component: met
  - Pace component: not met
  - Maximum-timeframe component: met
- **Aid restriction:** Fall 2026 federal grants, federal loans, and institutional grants are currently blocked.
- **Financial-aid review case:** Open and awaiting your response. The priority deadline is **September 18, 2026**.
- **Enrollment:** You are enrolled in 9 units. Your approved Reduced Course Load permits you to remain at 9 units for Academic Notice purposes. However, that approval **does not waive the 67% SAP requirement**. Also, 9 units is below the 12-unit full-time threshold, so some aid amounts could be adjusted even if your appeal is approved.

### What you need to do next

1. **Submit the assigned SAP appeal through Berkeley’s Financial Aid Forms process before September 18, 2026.** The form is available and the submission window is open.
2. Include:
   - A letter explaining the circumstances that caused your academic difficulty and failed course;
   - What has changed;
   - Specific steps and resources you will use to improve;
   - Supporting documentation, if available;
   - Your Academic Plan.
3. Make sure the Academic Plan is fully reviewed, approved, and signed by:
   - Your **L&S College Adviser**, and
   - Your **Undergraduate Major Adviser** in Cognitive Science.  
   Your record shows College Adviser review, but it does not show major-adviser approval yet.
4. Complete the L&S academic recovery process by **December 18, 2026**. Follow the plan, take letter grades when available, and work toward at least a **2.0 term GPA** and **2.0 cumulative UC GPA**.
5. Do not drop below your approved 9-unit floor without speaking with L&S Advising first. Also contact Financial Aid before changing your enrollment because it may affect both the appeal plan and award amount.

Submitting the appeal does not guarantee approval, and aid generally remains blocked while the case is reviewed. Decisions may take several weeks after a complete submission.

Sources: [UC Berkeley SAP policy](https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/appeals-special-circumstances/satisfactory-academic-progress/), [L&S Academic Notice](https://lsadvising.berkeley.edu/academic-difficulty/academic-notice), and [Financial Aid eligibility and enrollment](https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/eligibility/).

final_answer:
Your fall aid is blocked because of **Satisfactory Academic Progress (SAP)**, not simply because you are on Academic Notice.

### What I found

- **Academic Notice:** Active for Fall 2026 after a prior-term GPA below 1.5 following a failed course.
- **Academic recovery plan:** In progress; student and College Adviser must complete it by **December 18, 2026**.
- **SAP result:** **Not met** because your cumulative completion rate is **64.9%**—48 completed units out of 74 attempted. Berkeley requires at least **67%**.
  - GPA component: met
  - Pace component: not met
  - Maximum-timeframe component: met
- **Aid restriction:** Fall 2026 federal grants, federal loans, and institutional grants are currently blocked.
- **Financial-aid review case:** Open and awaiting your response. The priority deadline is **September 18, 2026**.
- **Enrollment:** You are enrolled in 9 units. Your approved Reduced Course Load permits you to remain at 9 units for Academic Notice purposes. However, that approval **does not waive the 67% SAP requirement**. Also, 9 units is below the 12-unit full-time threshold, so some aid amounts could be adjusted even if your appeal is approved.

### What you need to do next

1. **Submit the assigned SAP appeal through Berkeley’s Financial Aid Forms process before September 18, 2026.** The form is available and the submission window is open.
2. Include:
   - A letter explaining the circumstances that caused your academic difficulty and failed course;
   - What has changed;
   - Specific steps and resources you will use to improve;
   - Supporting documentation, if available;
   - Your Academic Plan.
3. Make sure the Academic Plan is fully reviewed, approved, and signed by:
   - Your **L&S College Adviser**, and
   - Your **Undergraduate Major Adviser** in Cognitive Science.  
   Your record shows College Adviser review, but it does not show major-adviser approval yet.
4. Complete the L&S academic recovery process by **December 18, 2026**. Follow the plan, take letter grades when available, and work toward at least a **2.0 term GPA** and **2.0 cumulative UC GPA**.
5. Do not drop below your approved 9-unit floor without speaking with L&S Advising first. Also contact Financial Aid before changing your enrollment because it may affect both the appeal plan and award amount.

Submitting the appeal does not guarantee approval, and aid generally remains blocked while the case is reviewed. Decisions may take several weeks after a complete submission.

Sources: [UC Berkeley SAP policy](https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/appeals-special-circumstances/satisfactory-academic-progress/), [L&S Academic Notice](https://lsadvising.berkeley.edu/academic-difficulty/academic-notice), and [Financial Aid eligibility and enrollment](https://financialaid.berkeley.edu/how-aid-works/eligibility/).
